summary: Joel Spolsky shares a grab bag of links and mini-reviews covering several topics: Chandler's first release, a proposal to replace SMTP with an authenticated protocol using micropayments to kill spam, and reviews of three books. He endorses Waltzing With Bears for its risk management framing of the Denver airport software 'failure,' recommends Speed Up Your Site for web optimization, and pans No-Collar as a muddled attempt to critique humane workplaces that fails because the author didn't believe his own thesis.
key_points: Joel supports replacing SMTP entirely with a protocol featuring authentication and micropayments, arguing that if the EU can switch currencies overnight, we can switch email protocols with a two-year deadline.

He praises Waltzing With Bears for reframing the Denver airport software delay as a risk management failure rather than a software engineering failure, noting the team was expected to perform the impossible.

He criticizes No-Collar for being a thesis in search of evidence — the author wanted to write about cool dotcoms but was stuck arguing their failure was caused by humane workplaces, which he didn't actually believe.
Joel notes the book would have been far better as an anthropological ethnography rather than an American Studies current-events piece.
money_quotes: If the European Union can change the money that 300 million people use all on one day, we can change our email protocol.
Let's find a protocol with decent authentication and with micropayments to make spam uneconomical, and let's set a deadline, maybe two years in the future, when SMTP will simply be turned off.
Makes a brave case that the person (or company, or team) who is responsible for risk management is the person who is going to pay the price when things go wrong.
It's pretty clear that the software team was expected to perform the impossible, and the project as a whole had no way to recover from a slip in a typically over-optimistic software schedule.

The author wanted to write a book about cool dotcoms, but they all imploded, and he was stuck trying to claim that they imploded because they tried to have a humane workplace, without really believing it himself, so it comes out a big mess.
key_insight: Software project failures are often risk management failures in disguise — the teams are set up to fail by organizations that have no plan for recovering from optimistic schedules.
